coredata.dc:title: A novel D-A-D-typed rod-like fluorescent material for efficient Fe(Ⅲ) and Cr(Ⅵ) detection: Synthesis, structure and properties
coredata.dc:creator: Qian, Junning
Huang, Ni
Lu, Qingyi
Wen, Can
Xia, Jiangbin
coredata.dc:description: Rod-like fluorescent material (4,7-di-pyrrol-1-yl-benzo[1,2,5]thiadiazole) with D-A-D structure is successfully designed and synthesized by introducing functional pyrrole group on benzothiadiazole molecule. Theoretical and experimental methods are adopted to deeply analyze its electronic structure, microstructure and optical properties. Upon the addition of Fe(Ⅲ) or Cr(Ⅵ), it immediately shows fluorescence quenching phenomenon while the addition of other ions exhibits no obvious quenching effect. The corresponding results reveal that DPYBT is quite promising for the fluorescence detection of Fe(Ⅲ) and Cr(Ⅵ), and the limit of detection for them are 3.04 μM and 43.5 nM, respectively. Meanwhile, it is practical for the visible detection in real environment. It also performs a great potential in search of new drugs towards 4DDM protein.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent material
D-A-D
Fluorescence quenching
Fe(Ⅲ)
Cr(Ⅵ)
Visible detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electron localization function, localized orbital locator and electrostatic potential analysis (ELF, LOL and ESP)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Calculated energy of DPYBT with different rotated angles; (b) Optimized structure of DPYBT with the lowest energy under B3LYP/6-31G* set.

(a) Electron localization function of DPYBT; (b) Localized orbital locator of DPYBT; (c) Electrostatic potential of DPYBT and (d) area percent in each ESP range. These DFT calculations are all based on B3LYP/6-31G* level.
FESEM images of DPYBT; (a)(b) in 10 μm size; (c)(d) in 2 μm size; (e)(f) in 1 μm size.

(a) UV–vis and FL spectra of DPYBT measured in THF solution; (b) CIE 1931(x,y) chromaticity diagram of UV–vis and FL spectra of DPYBT; (c) Photos of DPYBT under sunlight and UV-365 nm; (d) TG curve of DPYBT.
Frontier orbitals of DPYBT calculated at B3LYP/6-311 G (d, p) set.

(a) FL spectra of DPYBT in THF solution before and after adding different analytes (chlorides and K2Cr2O7), insert: corresponding fluorescence color changes (c(DPYBT) =5 μM), c(metal ions) =0.067 mM, aqueous solution)); (b) Relative fluorescence intensity with the addition of different analytes.

(a) FL spectra changes of DPYBT in THF solution treated with different Fe3+ concentrations (c(DPYBT) =10 μM); (b) FL spectra changes of DPYBT in THF solution treated with different Cr2O7 2− concentrations (c(DPYBT) =10 μM); (c) The relationship between different Fe3+ concentrations and their relative FL intensity. (Insert: linear relationship between DPYBT and Fe3+ (3.8 ∼ 7.2 μM)); (d) The relationship between different Cr2O7 2− concentrations and their relative FL intensity. (Insert: linear relationship between DPYBT and Cr2O7 2− (0.4 ∼ 1.1 μM)).
(a) FL intensity changes of DPYBT + Fe3+ in THF solution with different time (c(DPYBT) =10 μM); (b) FL intensity changes of DPYBT + Cr2O7 2− in THF solution with different time (c(DPYBT) =10 μM).

(a) FT-IR results of DPYBT, DPYBT + Fe3+ and DPYBT + Cr2O7 2−; (b) Calculated values for LUMO and HOMO of DPYBT and DPYBT-dimer under B3LYP/6-311G* level; (c) Description of LUMO and HOMO of DPYBT and DPYBT-dimer; (d) Fluorescence sensing mechanism of DPYBT with Fe3+ or Cr2O7 2−.
(a) Molecular docking of DPYBT with 4DDM protein; (b) Expanded region of red area in (a); (c) Molecular docking of DPYBT with 2LIG protein; (d) Expanded region of blue area in (c).

(a) Different DPYBT-based test papers prepared with various DPYBT concentrations (a: 1 μM; b: 2 μM, c: 3 μM, d: 5 μM, e: 10 μM); (b) DPYBT-based test papers made with the addition of different Fe3+ (μM), DPYBT-based test paper (e) is used as the blank; (c) DPYBT-based test papers made with the addition of different Cr2O7 2− (μM), DPYBT-based test paper (e) is used as the blank.
Properties of DPYBT obtained by experimental and theoretical methods.
Previous reports with Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2− probes.

head.ce:title: A novel D-A-D-typed rod-like fluorescent material for efficient Fe(Ⅲ) and Cr(Ⅵ) detection: Synthesis, structure and properties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Key Lab on Organic and Polymeric Optoelectronic Materials, College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Key Lab on Organic and Polymeric Optoelectronic Materials
College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Key Lab on Organic and Polymeric Optoelectronic Materials
College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rod-like fluorescent material (4,7-di-pyrrol-1-yl-benzo[1,2,5]thiadiazole) with D-A-D structure is successfully designed and synthesized by introducing functional pyrrole group on benzothiadiazole molecule. Theoretical and experimental methods are adopted to deeply analyze its electronic structure, microstructure and optical properties. Upon the addition of Fe(Ⅲ) or Cr(Ⅵ), it immediately shows fluorescence quenching phenomenon while the addition of other ions exhibits no obvious quenching effect. The corresponding results reveal that DPYBT is quite promising for the fluorescence detection of Fe(Ⅲ) and Cr(Ⅵ), and the limit of detection for them are 3.04 μM and 43.5 nM, respectively. Meanwhile, it is practical for the visible detection in real environment. It also performs a great potential in search of new drugs towards 4DDM protein.

ce:section.ce:para: Fe(Ⅲ) (iron ion), as a kind of heavy metal ion, plays a vital role in human body and environment [1,2]. For example, the methemoglobinemia disease is caused by the high content of iron ion in the body while the absence of iron ion will lead to the anemia disease and other symptoms. In addition, high Fe(Ⅲ) content in algal cells can also result in the variation of their morphology. And this change can increase the degree of eutrophication in lakes, which is a threat to the environment. Another highly toxic chemical (Cr(Ⅵ), mainly existing as Cr2O7 2−) is an indispensable matter that is used in the industrial process, such as in the preparation of paint and leather [3,4]. It is so soluble in water that it is easy to enter into the organism according to the water circulating system. It can cause a huge damage to the organism because of the effect of further accumulation through food chain. For instance, it is able to lead to the cell dysfunction and destroy cells. Seriously, it can further interact with DNA and cause gene mutation to some extent. Therefore, it is pretty significant to develop a new detection method for the recognition of Fe(Ⅲ) and Cr(Ⅵ).

Recently, developments of D-A-D-typed materials have aroused intense interest of researchers because of their wide optical band gaps, simple preparation and diversified structures [5–7]. These materials are widely used for biomedicine, organic optoelectronics, energy development and chemosensors [7–13]. Especially in the field of chemosensors, promoted sensing performance of materials can be achieved by enhancing the whole planarity of organic molecules through a reasonable molecular design on combining conjugated groups and fluorescent groups [14,15]. Among these D-A-D materials, benzothiadiazole group is often used as the acceptor unit in D-A-D structure because of its excellent optical properties and high electron transporting features [16]. However, the donor units in these D-A-D materials are mainly the units of furan, thiophene, EDOT, triarylamines and their derivatives [17–23]. Few studies on D-A-D-typed materials are focused on benzothiadiazole with pyrrole and its derivatives, not to mention their applications in chemosensors. This is due to the instability of pyrrole, which affects its use in the design of materials. Moreover, to the best of our knowledge, reports on D-A-D-typed materials as Fe(Ⅲ) and Cr(Ⅵ) chemosensors are quite less.

Herein, we have successfully synthesized a novel D-A-D-structured fluorescent material (4,7-di-pyrrol-1-yl-benzo[1,2,5]thiadiazole, denoted as DPYBT) based on the structural modification of benzothiadiazole molecule, which performs excellent fluorescence characteristics. The structure and properties of DPYBT are then analyzed by theoretical and experimental methods. Moreover, it is successfully applied as the fluorescent chemosensor for the recognition of Fe(Ⅲ) and Cr(Ⅵ), and the corresponding detection characteristics for Fe(Ⅲ) and Cr(Ⅵ) are excellent. Additionally, we further expand the application for DPYBT, and find it also possible in designing drugs.

The first step product (4,7-dibromo-1,2,5-benzothiadiazole) was synthesized according to the previous reports [24]. Later, the mixture of prepared product (100.0 mg, 0.34 mmol), pyrrole (47.0 mg, 0.70 mmol), CuI (64.8 mg, 0.34 mmol), K3PO4 (148.6 mg, 0.70 mmol) and ethylene diamine (13.8 mg, 0.23 mmol) was heated in dioxane in N2 atmosphere for 18 h. Then the corresponding composite was purified by column chromatography method. Finally, the bright yellow product 4,7-di-pyrrol-1-yl-benzo[1,2,5]thiadiazole (named as DPYBT) was obtained (yield: 85 %). The 1H NMR and 13C NMR results are displayed in Fig. S1. Related molecular structure and synthetic route of DPYBT are shown in Fig. 1 and Scheme 1 , respectively. 1H NMR: δ (400 MHz, CDCl3); 7.57 (s, 2 H); 7.55 (t, 4 H); 6.46 (t, 4 H); 13C NMR: δ (400 MHz, CDCl3); 141.0, 130.1, 121.4, 119.3, 110.8.

1H NMR and 13C NMR spectra were recorded on WNMR-I NMR 600 MHz Machine (Bruker Company, Germany). XRD pattern was collected by Bruker D8 Advance from 10° ∼ 30° (Bruker Company, Germany). FT-IR results were tested on FT-IR iS10 (Nicolet Company, USA). Morphology images of sample were gotten from FESEM (Carl Zeiss Company, England). Additionally, corresponding optical properties were collected with UV–vis (Shimadzu Company, Japan) and FL spectra (Shimadzu Company, Japan) in the THF solution. The stability of sample was characterized with Thermogravimetric Analyzer (HITACHI Company, Japan).

Optimized structures of DPYBT and DPYBT-dimer were obtained with B3LYP/6-31G* basis on Gaussian. B3LYP/6-311 G (d,p) basis was later used to find the LUMO and HOMO values. ELF, LOL and ESP analysis were conducted to obtain the information of molecular structure based on the optimized structure [25]. Optimized structure of DPYBT and Protein 4DDM and 2LIG were used to carry out the docking study on AutoDock software. Binding energy was then adopted to evaluate the docking results.

As a good method to clarify the molecular structure, theoretical calculation is extensively used to analyze the structure of materials. Fig. 2 (a) performs the distribution of molecular potential energy of DPYBT with the rotation of pyrrole unit. It can be seen that the molecular potential energy shows the largest when the rotation angle is 0°, 180° and 360°, which stands for the unstable conformations. The smallest molecular potential energy is located at 60°, 120°, 240° and 300°, indicating the structures in these places are pretty stable. Besides, it can be noted that the maximum difference of molecular energy among different structures is about 0.117 eV. In addition, Fig. 2(b) shows the optimized molecular structure of DPYBT, which corresponds to the structure with the smallest molecular potential energy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electron localization function, localized orbital locator and electrostatic potential analysis (ELF, LOL and ESP)
ce:section.ce:para: ELF is usually employed to investigate the properties of intramolecular chemical bonds. It can be seen from Fig. 3 (a) that the region with higher energy is mainly resulted from benzothiadiazole group and N atom of pyrrole, while the region of pyrrole unit without N atom shows relatively low energy. These results exhibit that the electrons of benzothiadiazole group and N atom in pyrrole are restricted in these areas, while the electrons in other regions are more active and easy to be delocalized to other positions. [25]

The spatial structure described by LOL in Fig. 3(b) is helpful to analyze the shell-shape and the state of lone electron. The area formed between bonding atoms is ascribed to the slowest electron, and the corresponding LOL value is more than 0.5. [26] Furthermore, those dark red areas are where the lone pair electrons appear. And the place which is surrounded by green color belongs to delocalized electrons (LOL = 0.5). It means that the binding force of the electrons in these regions is relatively weak and easy to break away from the molecular system. [27] It can be further inferred that the electrons of H atoms in pyrrole unit show strong delocalization [27]. The rest place represents the region of electron avoidance, such as the space away from nucleus.

ESP is widely used in the study of molecular surface activity. It can be seen from Fig. 3(c) that the region in blue (ESP < 0 kcal/mol) on DPYBT molecular surface is mainly distributed on pyrrole unit and N atoms of benzothiadiazole, and the rest regions in red belong to the positive ESP values. Besides, the ESP values on DPYBT surface are mainly concentrated between -20 kcal/mol and +10 kcal/mol (Fig. 3(d)), and the minimum and maximum values of ESP are located at N atoms and H atoms of benzothiadiazole, respectively. Most importantly, the active electrophilic site represented by negative ESP values on DPYBT molecular surface are very helpful to predict whether DPYBT is toxic to biological macromolecules. [28] This is because that the site can directly interact with biological macromolecules as the two N atoms of benzothiadiazole can serve as the primary and secondary electrophilic sites, which will play an crucial role in the connection of DPYBT and macromolecules.

XRD pattern of DPYBT is shown in Fig. S2(a), it exhibits several apparent diffraction peaks, which are mainly located at 10.79°, 13.59°, 17.43°, 18.22°, 21.69°, 23.12°. And the maximum d-spacing is found to be 0.819 nm. These results reflect that DPYBT exhibits a good crystal structure. [29] To observe the microstructure of DPYBT, FESEM is used. Fig. 4 (a) and (b) are the FESEM images of DPYBT under 10 μm. It can be seen that the prepared DPYBT mostly presents rod-like structure. Further observation in Fig. 4(c ∼ f) can be found that these particles are some regular and orderly cuboid morphology with excellent crystal characteristics, which is in agreement with XRD results. Moreover, the average sizes of these rod-like particles are between 700 nm ∼ 10 μm.

FT-IR is then performed to characterize the chemical bonds in DPYBT. As shown in Fig. 2S(b), major characteristic vibrations of chemical bonds are found in the region of 1130 ∼ 1820 cm−1 [30,31]. Besides, CH bonds in DPYBT are centred at 2957 ∼ 3202 cm-1 and 657 ∼ 855 cm−1, respectively. [32] The above results indicate that DPYBT with rod-like structure has been successfully prepared.

As shown in Fig. 5 (a), DPYBT shows two absorption peaks at 295 nm and 417 nm, which is the result of π-π* transition. [33]. Relevant molar absorption coefficient of DPYBT is calculated as 6715 M−1 cm−1 in Table 1 , and the acquired band gap is 3.716 eV by Tauc plot in Fig. S3. In addition, it displays a strong fluorescence emission peak at 553 nm. This strong fluorescence is mainly attributed to the excellent π-π* conjugated structure in DPYBT as well. [34] And a corresponding excitation peak is found at 436 nm (Table 1 and Fig. S4(a)). The above studies reveal that DPYBT possesses a larger Stokes shift of 5898 cm-1 (Table 1), which is quite suitable as the fluorescent probe because of the advantages of low background interference, small light damage to biological samples and high detective sensitivity. [35] Moreover, available data in Fig. 5(b) also shows that the CIE value of DPYBT changes from CIE (0.244, 0.182) to CIE (0.407, 0.574) when it transitions from ground state to excited state and then back to ground state. Impressively, Fig. 5(c) illustrates the luminescence phenomenon that DPYBT presents orange color to naked eyes in sunlight while it manifests strong yellow fluorescence under UV lamp-365 nm.

DFT calculation is subsequently used to deeply gain insight into the nature of optical properties of DPYBT. The calculated values of HOMO and LUMO are -6.159 eV and -2.823 eV, accordingly. In addition, calculated dipole moment of DPYBT is 1.33 D (Table 1), revealing a relatively weak polarity compared to water molecule. Simultaneously, obtained information of frontier orbitals and PDOS in Fig. S5 and Fig. 6 all demonstrate that HOMO level of DPYBT is formed with the transition of benzothiadiazole-like HOMO and pyrrole-like HOMO-1, while the LUMO level is composed of benzothiadiazole-like LUMO. This HOMO → LUMO transition accounts for the absorption peak in UV–vis region (around 345 nm ∼ 550 nm). [36] Another adsorption peak at 295 nm caused from HOMO-1 → LUMO + 1 transition is linked to the transition among pyrrole-like HOMO-1, pyrrole-like LUMO + 1 and benzothiadiazole-like LUMO + 1 [36].

Thermodynamic properties of DPYBT is investigated by TG analysis. As seen in Fig. 5(d), the weight loss of DPYBT can be mainly divided into two stages. The first stage is attributed to the structural destruction of DPYBT because of decomposition process, which primarily occurs from 150 °C to 500 °C. Related weight loss is about 81 %. This result manifests that DPYBT presents excellent thermal stability below 150 °C. After that, the further decrease is around 19 % weight loss. This stage is caused by the total decomposition of DPYBT, which is the result of forming CO2, NOx and SO2 [37].

Inspired by the excellent fluorescent properties of DPYBT, we then employ it as fluorescent probe toward metal ions detection. Fig. 7 (a) illustrates the FL spectra and color changes (UV light) of DPYBT are independently added with equal amounts of Na+, Cu2+, Cr2O7 2−, Ni2+, Mn2+, K+, Al3+, Fe3+, Sn2+, Co2+, Zn2+. Nearly, the fluorescence intensity of DPYBT changes little except adding Fe3+ or Cr2O7 2−. But the addition of Co2+, Ni2+ and Cu2+ can also weaken the fluorescence of DPYBT. This decrease caused by them is much smaller than the effect by the addition of Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2-. To some extent, it is because that these heavy metal ions can form a weak coordination structure with these N and S atoms in DPYBT, thus affecting its fluorescence. Interestingly, Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2- performs a greater reduction in its fluorescence, which means that there is a different mechanism between them and DPYBT.

According to Fig. 7(a) and (b), the fluorescence intensity of DPYBT is decreased to 60 % for Fe3+ and 73 % for Cr2O7 2−, and their corresponding yellow fluorescence is quenched. Fluorescent bleaching test in Fig. S4(b) further reveals the fluorescence intensity of DPYBT does not change with time, showing the quenching effect is caused by adding Fe3+ or Cr2O7 2−. These results indicate that DPYBT is highly sensitive for the recognition of Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2−.

So as to explore the detection performance of DPYBT probe for Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2−, fluorescence titration experiments between DPYBT probe and Fe3+ or Cr2O7 2− are carried out. As shown in Fig. 8 (a), the fluorescence intensity of DPYBT decreases with the increase of Fe3+ concentration. It can be found in Fig. 8(c) that there is a linear relationship between DPYBT and Fe3+ concentration when Fe3+ is in a low concentration range. According to following equation and the results in Fig. 8(c), [38–40] the LOD (limit of detection) is calculated as 3.04 μM.
LOD = 3 δ / K
(δ: standard deviation; K: slope)

Fig. 8(b) and (d) present the fluorescence titration results of DPYBT probe for Cr2O7 2−. Similar to the titration result of Fe3+, the fluorescence intensity of DPYBT is decreased when Cr2O7 2− concentration increases, and the fluorescence intensity of DPYBT also performs a good linear relationship with the low concentration range of Cr2O7 2−. The corresponding LOD is calculated as 43.5 nM. These LODs of DPYBT probe for Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2− are lower than those of many reported chemosensors in Table 2 . Therefore, we believe that this work will be quite meaningful for the development of high-performance chemosensors for the recognition of Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2−.

Time-dependent fluorescence intensity variation is further applied to analyze the sensing performance of DPYBT for Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2−. As shown in Fig. 9 (a) and (b), the fluorescence intensity of DPYBT decreases rapidly and then reaches equilibrium with the addition of a certain amount of Fe3+ or Cr2O7 2−, indicating DPYBT can serve also as a fast responsive fluorescence probe for sensing Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2− as well. Moreover, it is obvious that the effect of fluorescence quenching is better when the amount of Fe3+ or Cr2O7 2− is increased. This is because the increase of Fe3+ or Cr2O7 2− concentration is helpful for promoting the extent of reaction. By comparing Fig. 9(a) and (b), the addition of Cr2O7 2− can accelerate the speed of fluorescence quenching, which is much faster than the addition of Fe3+. This result is possibly ascribed to the stronger oxidation capacity of Cr2O7 2−.

To further analyze the fluorescence sensing mechanism of DPYBT for Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2−, we have carried out additional experiments and theoretical calculation. Fig. 10 (a) display the FT-IR spectra of DPYBT and samples that are acquired after the reaction of DPYBT + Fe3+ or DPYBT + Cr2O7 2−. It can be seen that the intensity of CH bonds are greatly weakened for the obtained samples, and their corresponding CC and CC bands perform the obvious aggregation state. These results indicate that DPYBT is polymerized in the existence of Fe3+ or Cr2O7 2−.And further observation in Fig. 10(a) exhibits that the degree of polymerization for the sample from DPYBT + Cr2O7 2− is much higher. This result is well in agreement with the time-dependent fluorescence analysis. Dimer model of DPYBT is subsequently utilized to analyze these changes after polymerization by DFT calculation. Related findings in Fig. 10(b) and (c) point out that the HOMO level is increased while LUMO level and corresponding band gap are all decreased when DPYBT is polymerized. These results are caused by the increase of barriers of hole-injection and electron-injection after polymerization. [50] Combining the results above, we make a simple description about the fluorescence quenching mechanism (Fig. 10(d)). The polymer is then formed when Fe3+ or Cr2O7 2- serves as the oxidant to polymerize DPYBT, the rotation of fluorescent group of DPYBT is limited because of the thermodynamic stability of the newly formed rigid chain. [51] The DPYBT with bright yellow fluorescence is subsequently quenched (Fig. S6).

The development of new drugs is a costly and long-term work. Molecular docking, as a computer-aided drug design technology, is now widely used in the investigation of searching new drugs. [52,53] Inspired by the electrostatic potential of DPYBT on the possible interaction with biological molecules, we then carry out related molecular docking simulation among DPYBT and 4DDM and 2LIG proteins. According to the prediction from stimulated results in Fig. 11 (a) and (b), DPYBT molecule interacts with the central position of 4DDM protein. It can be further found that there are many kinds of residues (VAL-172, LEU-177, ASN-176, ASP-178) around DPYBT molecule under docking state. In addition, a new interaction is achieved based on the hydrogen bond (bond length: 3.0 Å) between LEU-177 residue and DPYBT molecule, and this binding energy is subsequently obtained as −13.305 kJ/mol. Fig. 11(c) and (d) express that DPYBT molecule interacts with the edge region of 2LIG protein, and there are also many residues (MET-75, ALA-78, ASN-80, GLN-81, SER-83) around DPYBT molecule as well. Meanwhile, a hydrogen bond (bond length: 3.2 Å) between SER-83 residue and DPYBT molecule appears, and relevant binding energy is −12.092 kJ/mol. It is found that the binding energy between DPYBT and 4DDM is greater than that between DPYBT and 2LIG, indicating that DPYBT molecule is more suitable for the development of new drugs towards 4DDM protein.

Paper test is a convenient method for visible analysis in real environment. [54] The excellent fluorescence detection performance of DPYBT makes it possible in this field. Paper-based samples are later prepared by soaking and drying DPYBT on filter papers. As shown in Fig. 12 (a), the fluorescent color of prepared paper changes from light gray to bright yellow with the increase of DPYBT concentration. Similar to pH paper, prepared DPYBT paper (e) is also able to determine the concentration of Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2−. Fig. 12(b) and (c) indicate that the fluorescence color of paper sheet (e) gradually changes from bright yellow to dark yellow, and finally it is quenched when 2.0 μM Fe3+ or 0.2 μM Cr2O7 2− is used for the visible fluorescence detection. Moreover, it is apparent that the quenching degree of Cr2O7 2− to DPYBT is greater, which is consistent with the fluorescence titration results. The excellent detection performance promotes DPYBT probe to be potential in real environmental detection field as well.

In conclusion, a new D-A-D-typed fluorescent material (DPYBT) with rod-like structure has been well constructed. Theoretical and experimental methods are then employed together to describe the relationship between structure and properties. Impressively, the prepared DPYBT exhibits excellent performances in the recognition of Fe(Ⅲ) and Cr(Ⅵ), and limit of detection are 3.04 μM and 43.5 nM, accordingly. Additionally, the prepared DPYBT-based paper shows a great potential in visible detection field. Moreover, DPYBT also plays an important role in finding new drugs towards 4DDM protein. This paper is primarily concentrated on synthesizing a new pyrrole derivative for environmental and biological applications. In general, we believe that this kind of new material (DPYBT) will be quite promising in more applications.
Mr. Junning Qian carried out some experiments, calculation and wrote the manuscript draft.
Miss. Ni Huang synthesized the compound and carried out most experiments.
Mr. Qingyi Lu measured some of the experiments and analysis some of the results.
Mr. Can Wen carried out some of the experiment.
Dr. Xia designed the project, provided guidance, and polished the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work is supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (21875173), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2042019gf0028) of China and Large-scale Instrument And Equipment Sharing Foundation of Wuhan University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.128377.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal-organic framework as selectivity regulator for Fe3+ and ascorbic acid detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Guo, L.; Liu, Y.; Kong, R.M.; Chen, G.; Liu, Z.; Qu, F.L.; Xia, L.; Tan, W.H. A Metal-Organic Framework as Selectivity Regulator for Fe3+ and Ascorbic Acid Detection. Anal. Chem. 2019, 91, 12453-12460.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red fluorescent carbon dots with phenylboronic acid tags for quick detection of Fe(III) in PC12 cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Li, C.J.; Wang, Y.Q.; Zhang, X.J.; Guo, X.L.; Kang, X.X.; Du, L.B.; Liu, Y. Red fluorescent carbon dots with phenylboronic acid tags for quick detection of Fe(III) in PC12 cells. J. Colloid. Interf. Sci. 2018, 526, 487-496.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sensing of Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2- in water and white light: synthesis, characterization, and fluorescence properties of a crystalline bismuth-1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylic acid framework

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Sun, Y.; Zhang, N.; Guan, Q.L.; Liu, C.H.; Li, B.; Zhang, K.Y.; Li, G.H.; Xing, Y.H.; Bai, F.Y.; Sun, L.X. Sensing of Fe3+ and Cr2O72- in Water and White Light: Synthesis, Characterization, and Fluorescence Properties of a Crystalline Bismuth-1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylic Acid FrameworkAcid Framework. Cryst. Growth Des. 2019, 19, 7217-7229.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A durable luminescent ionic polymer for rapid detection and efficient removal of toxic Cr2O7 2-
